family_history: Mother: type 2 diabetes, systemic lupus erythematosus
No family history of epilepsy or psychiatric hospitalization

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, approximately 2-3 glasses of wine per week
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies any illicit drug use; last use of marijuana was over 3 years ago
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband in a single-family home
social_history.exercise: Runs 3 times per week, practices yoga

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but confused. Oriented to person only; unable to state the date, month, or current location. Speech is fluent but tangential with occasional loose associations. She intermittently becomes agitated and attempts to get out of bed. Attention is impaired; unable to spell WORLD backward or perform serial 7s. Short-term memory is significantly impaired; recalls 0 of 3 objects at 5 minutes. She intermittently appears to respond to internal stimuli, though it is unclear whether these represent hallucinations or brief seizures. Montreal Cognitive Assessment score estimated at 12/30 based on partial cooperation.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm bilaterally. Extraocular movements are full without nystagmus. Facial sensation is intact. There is subtle intermittent perioral twitching observed during examination, initially thought to be anxiety-related. Hearing is grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue is midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Tone is mildly increased in bilateral upper extremities. Strength is 5/5 in all extremities when the patient cooperates, though she is intermittently resistive and effort is inconsistent. No pronator drift is observed. Occasional involuntary flexion posturing of the left wrist is noted during periods of inattention.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick in all extremities. Proprioception is intact at the great toes bilaterally. Detailed sensory examination is limited by the patient's fluctuating cooperation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk), Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing shows mild bilateral dysmetria, though this may be confounded by inattention. Heel-to-shin testing is grossly intact on the right; the patient does not cooperate with left-sided testing.
neurological_exam.gait: Unsteady, wide-based gait. The patient requires assistance and tends to veer to the left. Tandem gait is not attempted due to safety concerns.
neurological_exam.additional: Intermittent perioral and lingual dyskinesias are observed, including subtle lip-pursing and tongue movements. These are initially attributed to anxiety by the referring psychiatrist. One episode of behavioral arrest with staring, lip smacking, and right hand automatisms lasting approximately 20 seconds is witnessed during the examination, followed by brief post-ictal confusion superimposed on baseline encephalopathy. Mild diaphoresis is noted. Heart rate fluctuates between 88 and 115 bpm during the encounter.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive confusion, insomnia, and one witnessed seizure over three weeks; initially evaluated as psychiatric
patient.history_present_illness: This 28-year-old right-handed woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband with progressive confusion, behavioral changes, and a witnessed seizure. Approximately three weeks ago, her husband noticed she was sleeping poorly, lying awake for most of the night despite no change in her routine or medication. Over the following days, she became increasingly irritable and emotionally labile, crying without provocation and expressing paranoid ideation that her coworkers were trying to get her fired. She called in sick to work repeatedly and stopped exercising. Her husband initially attributed the changes to stress, as her school year had been demanding. Ten days prior to this presentation, she was evaluated at an urgent care and referred to her psychiatrist, who increased her sertraline to 100 mg daily and prescribed lorazepam 0.5 mg as needed for acute anxiety. Despite this, her behavior continued to deteriorate. She began having difficulty following conversations, frequently losing her train of thought mid-sentence, and on several occasions she was found staring blankly for 10-15 seconds before resuming activity, which she could not recall. Five days ago, her husband witnessed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 60 seconds while she was sitting on the couch. He called 911, and she was taken to a community hospital emergency department where a CT head was unremarkable. She was given a loading dose of levetiracetam 1500 mg IV and discharged with a prescription for levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily and instructions to follow up with neurology. Over the next four days, her confusion worsened substantially. She became unable to manage simple household tasks, left the stove on twice, and had difficulty recognizing familiar neighborhood landmarks while being driven. Her husband also noticed unusual repetitive mouth movements that she seemed unaware of. Today, she became acutely agitated, was found attempting to leave the house in her pajamas in the rain, and did not recognize her own mother who had come to help. Her husband brought her to this tertiary care emergency department for further evaluation. He denies any recent fevers, headaches, or focal weakness. There is no history of head trauma, recent travel, tick exposure, or new sexual contacts. Her psychiatric history is limited to anxiety managed with low-dose sertraline; she has never been hospitalized for psychiatric illness and has no prior psychotic episodes.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: Poorly organized 7 Hz theta activity over the occipital regions, replacing expected alpha rhythm

findings.clinical_correlation: Indicates diffuse cerebral dysfunction; non-specific but consistent with encephalitis or metabolic encephalopathy

findings.frequency: Seen intermittently, approximately 2-3 per minute during the recording
findings.morphology: Sharply contoured theta-frequency waves with aftergoing slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptiform potential; correlates with the patient's clinical seizure and suggests a temporal lobe epileptogenic focus

findings.location: Left temporal onset with brief spread to left hemisphere
findings.frequency: One subclinical electrographic seizure captured, lasting 35 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic 5-6 Hz theta evolving to repetitive sharp waves
findings.state: Correlated with observed behavioral arrest and lip smacking
findings.clinical_correlation: Confirms focal seizures of temporal origin; consistent with encephalitis-related epileptogenicity

artifacts.description: Intermittent temporal muscle artifact bilaterally, partially obscuring temporal chains during periods of agitation

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed; patient unable to cooperate
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photic driving response; no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG demonstrating moderate diffuse background slowing, generalized rhythmic delta activity with frontal predominance, intermittent left temporal sharp waves, and one captured electrographic seizure of left temporal origin. The findings indicate diffuse cortical dysfunction with an epileptogenic focus in the left temporal region. The overall pattern is consistent with an encephalitic process. Notably, the extreme delta brush pattern is not identified on this recording, though this pattern may not be present in all cases or at all disease stages.
eeg.limitations: 30-minute routine EEG; continuous EEG monitoring is recommended for more comprehensive seizure burden assessment
recommended_actions: Continuous EEG monitoring for at least 24-48 hours
Consider autoimmune encephalitis workup given clinical context

findings.location: Left medial temporal lobe, involving the hippocampus and amygdala

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity, could represent artifact vs. early pathology

additional_observations: Ventricles are normal in size and configuration
mri.impression: Subtle FLAIR hyperintensity in the left medial temporal lobe without restricted diffusion, enhancement, or mass effect. The finding is equivocal and could represent early inflammatory pathology versus artifact. Clinical correlation is recommended. The absence of hemorrhagic changes or restricted diffusion makes HSV encephalitis less likely, but an autoimmune or early viral encephalitis cannot be excluded.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Subtle unilateral medial temporal signal change; often bilateral but can be asymmetric or unilateral early in the course
Temporal lobe involvement but typically shows restricted diffusion and hemorrhagic changes in HSV
The finding is subtle enough to represent artifact; follow-up imaging may be warranted
No hippocampal volume loss or atrophy to suggest chronic process

recommended_actions: Clinical and serological correlation, particularly autoimmune encephalitis panel
Consider repeat MRI in 7-10 days if clinical suspicion persists

BMP.clinical_significance: Mild hyponatremia; consider SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology

Other.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, possibly related to recent seizure activity
Consistent with prescribed lorazepam; no unexpected substances detected

labs.interpretation: Mild hyponatremia (133 mEq/L) possibly secondary to SIADH in the setting of CNS pathology, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, and mildly elevated CK likely from seizure activity. Normal thyroid function helps exclude Hashimoto encephalopathy. Urine drug screen positive for benzodiazepines is accounted for by prescribed lorazepam. Normal hepatic and renal function. CBC is unremarkable.
abnormal_values_summary: Urine drug screen positive for benzodiazepines (prescribed)

special_tests.Cytology: Rare reactive lymphocytes, no malignant cells
special_tests.Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies: Pending (sent to reference laboratory, expected turnaround 5-7 business days)

csf.interpretation: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (12 cells/uL, 88% lymphocytes) with borderline elevated protein. Glucose ratio is normal. Oligoclonal bands are equivocal and IgG index is borderline elevated. All viral PCRs are negative, making active viral encephalitis unlikely. Bacterial cultures show no growth. Anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing has been sent to a reference laboratory and results are pending. The mild CSF inflammatory profile does not exclude autoimmune encephalitis, as early cases may show minimal abnormalities. Clinical correlation and follow-up antibody results are essential.

NMDAR Antibody Panel.clinical_significance: Positive in serum; however, CSF testing is more sensitive and specific
CSF titer exceeds serum titer, confirming intrathecal antibody production. Diagnostic of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

output.interpretation: Both serum and CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies have returned positive. The CSF titer (1:128) exceeds the serum titer (1:80), confirming intrathecal antibody production. These results establish the diagnosis of anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Notably, the titers are moderate, consistent with the relatively early presentation and mild CSF pleocytosis seen on initial lumbar puncture. First-line immunotherapy should be initiated without delay.

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal, 3.0 x 2.2 x 2.3 cm with multiple small follicles consistent with polycystic morphology
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Polycystic morphology consistent with known PCOS; no teratoma identified

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: 3.4 x 2.5 x 2.8 cm, contains a 1.2 cm complex mass with mixed echogenicity including a hyperechoic focus with posterior acoustic shadowing suggesting calcification. Mild internal vascularity on Doppler.
Pelvic Ultrasound.clinical_significance: Suspicious for mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst); associated with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in young women

Pelvic Ultrasound.value: Normal size and echotexture, endometrial stripe 8 mm
Small amount of physiologic free fluid in the cul-de-sac

output.interpretation: A 1.2 cm complex mass in the left ovary with mixed echogenicity and calcification is suspicious for a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). The right ovary demonstrates polycystic morphology consistent with the patient's known PCOS but no mass lesion. The left ovarian teratoma, if confirmed, may represent the immunologic trigger for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Cross-sectional imaging (CT or MRI pelvis) is recommended for further characterization, and gynecologic oncology consultation should be considered for possible surgical resection.
abnormal_values_summary: Left ovary: 1.2 cm complex mass suspicious for mature cystic teratoma

Autoimmune Encephalitis Panel.clinical_significance: Low-titer ANA is common in the general population and in family members of SLE patients; not diagnostic of systemic autoimmune disease in isolation

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis panel confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibody positivity in CSF. All other neuronal surface antibodies are negative. Anti-TPO is normal, ruling out Hashimoto encephalopathy. ANA is weakly positive at 1:80 with a speckled pattern, which is a common incidental finding and is not diagnostic of systemic lupus in the absence of other clinical criteria. The patient's family history of SLE may account for this finding. Overall, the results support isolated anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.

signal_characteristics.density: Mixed density with fat component (-30 HU) and small calcific focus

output.impression: A 1.4 cm well-circumscribed left ovarian mass with fat and calcification components is consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst). No evidence of other primary malignancy, lymphadenopathy, or distant disease. The teratoma is the likely immunologic trigger for the patient's anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Bilateral polycystic ovarian morphology is consistent with known PCOS.

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Fat and calcification within a well-circumscribed ovarian mass is characteristic
Fat component and calcification are not typical of endometrioma

recommended_actions: Gynecologic oncology consultation for surgical resection

cell_count.WBC: 28 cells/uL (elevated, increased from 12 on prior LP)

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive (4 bands, not present in serum; increased from equivocal on prior LP)

output.interpretation: Repeat lumbar puncture shows worsening lymphocytic pleocytosis (28 cells/uL, up from 12) and rising protein. Oligoclonal bands are now definitively positive with 4 bands, and IgG index has increased, indicating progressive intrathecal immune activation. Anti-NMDA receptor antibodies remain positive at 1:128. The evolving CSF profile confirms active autoimmune encephalitis and supports the need for aggressive immunotherapy. The worsening CSF parameters are consistent with the clinical trajectory.

background.overall_organization: Severely disorganized, worse than routine EEG
background.asymmetry: Left temporal emphasis of epileptiform discharges

findings.location: Left temporal (T3-T5), with occasional spread to left frontal
findings.morphology: Sharp waves with aftergoing slow wave
findings.clinical_correlation: Epileptogenic focus consistent with temporal lobe involvement in encephalitis

findings.frequency: Three seizures captured over 24 hours, each lasting 30-90 seconds
findings.morphology: Rhythmic theta evolving to repetitive sharp waves with brief secondary generalization in one episode
findings.state: Two during wakefulness, one during sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Subclinical seizure burden significant; supports need for anti-seizure medication optimization

findings.morphology: Moderate amplitude periodic discharges with triphasic morphology
findings.clinical_correlation: Suggests diffuse cortical dysfunction; must be differentiated from metabolic encephalopathy in clinical context

output.impression: Continuous EEG over 24 hours demonstrates progressive background deterioration with diffuse slowing, frequent left temporal epileptiform discharges, three subclinical electrographic seizures of left temporal origin, and intermittent generalized periodic discharges. The extreme delta brush pattern is not identified. The evolving EEG findings are consistent with progressive encephalitis. The subclinical seizure burden is significant and warrants anti-seizure medication adjustment.
recommended_actions: Increase levetiracetam dose or add second anti-seizure medication
Urgent immunotherapy if not already initiated

Osmolality Studies.clinical_significance: Inappropriately concentrated relative to low serum osmolality, supports SIADH
Upper end of normal; in context of hyponatremia, supports impaired free water excretion consistent with SIADH

output.interpretation: Hypotonic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine is consistent with SIADH, a recognized complication of CNS inflammation in anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. Fluid restriction and serial sodium monitoring are recommended. Avoid rapid overcorrection to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.

differential.diagnosis: Primary psychiatric disorder (first-break psychosis with catatonic features)
differential.key_distinguishing: The patient's pre-existing anxiety diagnosis and initial psychiatric presentation with paranoia, insomnia, and behavioral changes led to initial psychiatric management. However, the subsequent development of seizures, CSF pleocytosis, and EEG abnormalities distinguishes this from primary psychiatric illness. Pure psychiatric disorders do not cause CSF pleocytosis or electrographic seizures.

Temporal lobe involvement on MRI overlaps with HSV encephalitis, but HSV typically presents more acutely with high fever, has hemorrhagic changes and restricted diffusion on MRI, and shows positive HSV PCR in CSF. All viral PCRs are negative in this case, and the subacute 3-week course is more typical of autoimmune encephalitis.

Can present with encephalopathy, seizures, and CSF pleocytosis. Distinguished by markedly elevated anti-TPO antibodies and thyroid dysfunction. This patient has normal thyroid function and normal anti-TPO antibodies. Family history of autoimmune disease raises the consideration but does not confirm this diagnosis.

Recent sertraline dose increase could theoretically contribute, but serotonin syndrome typically presents with clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia, and diarrhea rather than subacute encephalopathy with seizures and dyskinesias. CSF should be normal in serotonin syndrome.

Family history of SLE and low-titer ANA raise this possibility. However, the patient lacks other SLE criteria (no rash, arthritis, serositis, cytopenias), and the ANA titer is too low to be diagnostic. Anti-NMDAR antibody positivity provides a more specific diagnosis.

optimal_actions.action: Stabilize patient: ensure IV access, continuous cardiac and pulse oximetry monitoring, seizure precautions. Administer lorazepam 2 mg IV if active seizure.
Obtain comprehensive labs: CBC, BMP, CRP, ESR, CK, hepatic panel, thyroid function, urine drug screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia, mildly elevated inflammatory markers and CK, normal thyroid function, urine drug screen positive only for prescribed benzodiazepines

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle left medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity, equivocal but warranting clinical correlation

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF sent for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, bacterial culture, viral PCRs (HSV, VZV, enterovirus), oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cytology, and anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, borderline protein, equivocal oligoclonal bands, negative viral PCRs, NMDAR antibodies pending

optimal_actions.action: Start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h pending HSV PCR results
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Empiric coverage until HSV is excluded

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG; if abnormal, initiate continuous EEG monitoring for 24-48 hours
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Diffuse slowing, left temporal sharp waves, possible electrographic seizures without extreme delta brush

optimal_actions.action: Optimize anti-seizure medication: increase levetiracetam to 1000 mg IV q12h given breakthrough seizure activity
Given high clinical suspicion for autoimmune encephalitis despite pending antibodies, initiate empiric first-line immunotherapy with IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 5 days
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Early treatment initiation may improve outcomes; do not wait for antibody confirmation when clinical suspicion is high

optimal_actions.action: Add IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day for 5 days as part of first-line immunotherapy once NMDAR antibodies confirmed or if clinical deterioration continues
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Combined immunotherapy with steroids and IVIG or plasma exchange

optimal_actions.action: Order pelvic ultrasound to screen for ovarian teratoma
Consult gynecologic oncology for teratoma resection once confirmed on imaging
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Surgical planning for left ovarian teratoma resection to remove immunologic trigger

optimal_actions.action: Implement fluid restriction for SIADH-associated hyponatremia with serial sodium monitoring every 6-8 hours
If insufficient clinical improvement after 2 weeks of first-line immunotherapy: escalate to second-line therapy with rituximab 375 mg/m2 weekly for 4 doses
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improved neurological function with B-cell depletion

critical_actions: Perform lumbar puncture and send CSF for anti-NMDA receptor antibodies
Start empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR returns negative
Recognize that the clinical presentation (psychiatric symptoms progressing to seizures and dyskinesias) is suspicious for autoimmune encephalitis despite initial psychiatric framing
Initiate immunotherapy empirically based on clinical suspicion rather than delaying for antibody results
Screen for ovarian teratoma with pelvic imaging
Obtain continuous EEG monitoring to assess subclinical seizure burden
Admit to a monitored setting (ICU or step-down) given seizures and autonomic instability
contraindicated_actions: Accepting the psychiatric framing at face value and discharging with psychiatric follow-up only
Administering high-dose antipsychotics (especially first-generation), which can worsen dyskinesias and risk neuroleptic malignant syndrome
Delaying immunotherapy for days while awaiting antibody results from reference laboratory, when clinical suspicion is high
Attributing seizures solely to sertraline dose change and failing to pursue encephalitis workup
Rapid sodium correction exceeding 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours, risking osmotic demyelination syndrome
Performing brain biopsy when antibody testing can establish the diagnosis non-invasively
key_reasoning_points: The progression from psychiatric symptoms to seizures and movement disorder over 3 weeks in a young woman should trigger consideration of autoimmune encephalitis, even when the initial framing is psychiatric
Pre-existing anxiety disorder and recent sertraline dose increase create a plausible but misleading psychiatric narrative that may delay the correct diagnosis
Subtle or absent MRI findings do not exclude anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis; up to 50% of cases have normal MRI
Mild CSF pleocytosis (WBC 5-20) is common early in the disease course; a near-normal CSF does not rule out the diagnosis
The absence of extreme delta brush on EEG does not exclude NMDAR encephalitis; this pattern is seen in only about 30% of cases
Hyponatremia from SIADH is a common complication of CNS inflammation and should prompt further investigation
Family history of autoimmune disease (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis) increases pre-test probability for autoimmune conditions including autoimmune encephalitis
Ovarian teratoma screening is essential even with small or indeterminate masses, as tumor resection significantly improves outcomes and reduces relapse
Empiric immunotherapy should be started based on clinical suspicion when antibody results will take days to return, as delays worsen prognosis

metadata.author: NeuroBench Generator
metadata.difficulty_justification: Moderate difficulty due to initial psychiatric framing, subtle MRI findings, mild CSF pleocytosis, absence of extreme delta brush on EEG, and pending antibody results requiring clinical judgment to initiate empiric therapy
